dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for breathing problems.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I'm short of breath. I have asthma, and it's gotten really bad today.
How long has your shortness of breath been going on?
It's been about 30 minutes now, maybe a little longer.
Can I ask you for your name and birthday?
My name is Maria, and my birthday is March 14th.
Do you know which city and county you're in?
I'm in Richmond, in the county of Henrico.
What is today's date?
Today is the 8th of February.
What happened earlier to cause the 911 call?
I was petting my son's dog and then started cooking. Then I couldn't breathe.
Are you oriented to person, place, and time, and to why you called 911?
I know who I am, where I am, what day it is, and why I called for help.
When exactly did the breathing problems start?
When I was petting the dog. I think it triggered my asthma.
Did anything else happen after that?
I did three nebulizers already. It didn't help much.
Are you in any pain, or just tightness in your chest?
Tightness in the chest, not actual pain.
On a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst, how bad is the tightness?
I would say it's around a 4.
Is there anything in your throat or mouth that feels blocked?
No, nothing in my throat.
Can you speak in full sentences?
I can, but I still feel like I can't get in enough air.
Ma'am, we'll check your breathing now.
Are you having trouble getting a breath in or out?
It feels like I can't get a breath out.
What is your respiratory rate approximately?
Respiratory rate is 18.
Pulse is 135, strong and regular.
Blood pressure is 138 over 89.
Are you light-headed or dizzy?
No, I'm not light-headed or dizzy.
SpO2 is at 98%.
Glucose reading is not available; we'll follow up in the hospital for this information.
Still 138 over 89.
Pulse is now 94, still strong.
Do you have any past medical history we should know about?
I have moderate asthma. That's it.
What medications are you currently taking?
Albuterol and I also use nebulizers.
Allergies?
Codeine and Guaifenesin.
Your skin color, tone, and temperature-any current issues?
No. I feel normal.
Do you feel any pain in your head or neck?
No head pain or neck pain.
Equal chest rise and fall with wheezing on expiratory breaths.
Breathing sounds are diminished on the left.
Would you mind if I applied a 12-lead EKG?
Go ahead.
12-lead EKG obtained. Shows sinus tachycardia, possible left atrial enlargement, and a short QT interval noted on readout.
You are alert and oriented to all four aspects (person, place, time, situation). GCS is 15, AOX4 confirmed.
Based on the condition, we're following Protocol 3-16 for asthmatic failure.
IV started in the left arm, successful on first attempt with a 20-gauge catheter, despite challenges with flush after insertion.
Ma'am, are you awake enough for a nebulizer?
As capable as I can be during this issue.
Now nebulizing with 5mg Albuterol and 0.5mg Ipratropium, oxygen at 8LPM to help improve airway flow and ease your breathing.
We will now give a second dose of nebulized Albuterol at 5mg as a follow-up to the first dose for sustained relief of symptoms.
IM Dexamethasone, 10mg, is now administered for longer anti-inflammatory effects on the airway and to support nebulizer efforts in asthma exacerbation management.
SpO2 is now at 96% post-medication administration.
Blood pressure is now 164 over 79.
Heart rate is 94, possibly from initial relief from airway interventions.
We are going to safely move you onto a stair chair for ease of transfer from this location to the transport unit below. Are you ready for this step?
Yes, go ahead and be as careful as you can with my comfort in mind.
The chair is now securely latched in place and fitted around the lower body to allow smooth descent down the stairs. You'll be comfortable during the remainder of the procedure.
Patient is secured on the stretcher and placed in a semi-Fowler's position-this will help with easier breathing and offer you a more comfortable experience.
We will now be transporting you to Chippenham Hospital for further care, as they are best equipped to manage your asthma exacerbation.
That makes sense. I appreciate the care I received so far.

Hospital has been notified of your arrival and was given an initial report while we were descending the steps to get you ready for en route communication for seamless handover to their team upon arrival in the ED.
Vitals before transport: pulse 94, BP 164/79, SpO2 96%, all within reason for continued management of asthma exacerbation post-intervention stabilization efforts prior to transport now.
Respiratory status is stable with reduction in wheezing and improved airway function as noted after medication administration.
EKG confirms sinus tachycardia, possible left atrial enlargement, and a short QT interval as previously interpreted.
You mentioned before that you feel unwell. How are you now with the medications and transport preparations?
Still a bit like a sick person, but I'm more stable now with the medications you've given.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate your current chest tightness now that we've given you the medications?
I think it is around a 2 now with improved breathing and less tightness.
All systems go for departure. The patient is secure, vitals are stable, medications have been administered, and we are prepared to transport for ED evaluation commencing now.
Initial information including medications given has been relayed to the hospital at this time ahead of arrival to Chippenham for handover to the ED team upon arrival.
The patient has been fully assessed, treated in line with current EMS protocols and field impressions have been shared with the hospital for seamless handover upon arrival.
Transfer has been signed off by the on-site nurse, and all pre-hospital interventions and information have been relayed to the receiving facility without issue.
